diagnostics.message: `pow(x, 5.0)` should be written as `(x * x) * (x * x) * x` (pow-by-squaring; 3 multiplies vs. transcendental) — `pow` lowers to a transcendental sequence on every current GPU
`pow(x, 5.0)` should be replaced with a pow-by-squaring chain -- `pow` lowers to a transcendental sequence on every current GPU, but a handful of multiplies is strictly cheaper
`pow(2.0, x)` should be written as `exp2(x)` -- `exp2` is a single GPU instruction; `pow(2, x)` typically lowers to log2 + multiply + exp2
`pow(x, 2.0)` should be written as `x * x` — `pow` lowers to a transcendental sequence on every current GPU
`pow(x, 2.0)` should be written as `x * x` -- `pow` lowers to a transcendental sequence on every current GPU
`pow(x, 3.0)` should be written as `x * x * x` — `pow` lowers to a transcendental sequence on every current GPU
`pow(x, 3.0)` should be written as `x * x * x` -- `pow` lowers to a transcendental sequence on every current GPU
`pow(x, 5.0)` should be written as `(x * x) * (x * x) * x` (pow-by-squaring; 3 multiplies vs. transcendental) — `pow` lowers to a transcendental sequence on every current GPU
`pow(x, 5.0)` should be replaced with a pow-by-squaring chain -- `pow` lowers to a transcendental sequence on every current GPU, but a handful of multiplies is strictly cheaper
`1.0 / sqrt(x)` should be written as `rsqrt(x)` -- `rsqrt` is a single hardware instruction on every modern GPU
`lerp(a, b, 0)` always returns `a` — the interpolant is 0, so the result is the first endpoint
`lerp(a, b, 1)` always returns `b` — the interpolant is 1, so the result is the second endpoint
multiply by 1 is a no-op
adding 0 is a no-op
multiply by 0 is 0 for finite values, but preserves NaN for floats — use `0` directly only if the operand cannot be NaN
`sin(angle)` and `cos(angle)` are both present — use `sincos(angle, s, c)` to compute both for the cost of one transcendental instruction
`v - 2 * dot(n, v) * n` is the reflect formula — use `reflect(v, n)`
`length(a - b)` is the open-coded form of `distance(a, b)` -- prefer the named intrinsic for clarity
`x > threshold ? 1.0 : 0.0` is the `step` idiom — note: `step(threshold, x)` uses `>=` semantics; verify the boundary behaviour is correct
hand-rolled cubic Hermite interpolation — `n = saturate((t - a) / (b - a)); n * n * (3 - 2 * n)` is `smoothstep(a, b, t)`
`length(v)` compared against a scalar can usually be rewritten as `dot(v, v)` against the squared scalar -- saves a sqrt at the cost of an earlier overflow point
exact `==` on floating-point values is unreliable — rounding error and NaN make the result unpredictable; use an explicit tolerance such as `abs(a - b) < epsilon` instead
comparison with NaN literal `(0.0 / 0.0)` is always false (or always true for `!=`) per IEEE-754 — use `isnan(x)` instead
